original_abstract: Phenolic compounds are ubiquitous plant secondary metabolites that possess various biological activities and are known to interact with proteins, altering their structure and properties. Therefore, interactions between these compounds and proteins has gained increasing attention due to their potential benefits to human health and for exploitation by the food industry. Phenolic compounds and proteins can form complexes via covalent linkages and/ or non-covalent interactions through hydrophobic, electrostatic, van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding. This review describes possible mechanisms of phenol-protein complex formation, their physiological action and activi- ties that are important in the food industry, and possible outcomes in the terms of molecular docking and simula- tion analysis. The conformational changes of the protein upon binding with polyphenols can lead to the folding or unfolding of the protein molecules, forming insoluble or soluble complexes. The concentration of polyphenols, their molecular weight and structure, ions/cofactors and conditions of the system determine the precipitation or solubili- zation of the complex, affecting their nutritional and functional properties as well as their bioactivities. In this regard, molecular docking and simulation studies of phenolic-protein interactions allows comprehensive virtual screening of competitive/non-competitive and site-specific/non-specific conjugation of phenolics with different protein targets and facilitates understanding the observed effects. The docking analysis of flavonoids with enzymes and milk proteins has indicated their potential application in producing nutraceuticals and functional foods. Thus, combining molecular docking and simulation studies with experimental techniques is vital for better understanding the reactions that take place during digestion to engineer and manufacture novel food ingredients with desirable pharmacological proper- ties and as potential food additives.
humanized_abstract: Phenolic compounds represent a large group of plant secondary metabolites, rich in biological activities and known to bind protein structures by modifying their physical and biochemical properties. Thus, interest in phenolic compounds-protein interactions is growing at the scientific level, in relation to their health benefit effects and to their possible application in the food industry. Phenolic compounds can form covalent as well as non-covalent protein binding complexes via hydrophobic, electrostatic, van der Waals and hydrogen bonds. The current paper presents an overview of the possible interactions and mechanisms of formation, as well as the physiological role and significance and application in food industry of such complexes, based on empirical data related to molecular docking and simulation analyses. Upon binding with polyphenols, proteins undergo significant conformational changes, which may induce a new folded structure or unfolding of the protein molecule, leading thus to the formation of insoluble or soluble protein-polyphenol complexes. Their solubility-precipitation is a function of various parameters such as polyphenol concentration, molecular weight, type of structure, ions/cofactors or environmental conditions, which in turn influence their nutritional and functional characteristics and their bioactivity. By associating the results of molecular docking and simulation studies of the interactions involving various types of phenolic compounds and several proteins, it is possible to realize a more complete virtual screening of competitive/non-competitive site-specific/non-specific protein conjugation of various phenolic compounds with various enzyme/protein targets. For instance, a docking study of flavonoids with enzymes and milk proteins provided a large number of possible targets, opportunities for developing new nutraceutical and functional food products. Taking into account the experimental validation of protein-polyphenol interactions and their stability at different stages of food digestion, the combination of computational simulation (molecular docking) with experimental methods will allow a deeper and more complete understanding of the interactions taking place during digestion and will facilitate the design of new food ingredients (nutraceuticals) with required (desired) pharmacological properties and use as food additives.
